era_believed.statement: Saruman is the head of the Istari order, originally the most powerful of the five wizards sent to Middle-earth.
The tower of Orthanc at the center of Isengard is made of unbreakable black stone from the early days of Gondor.
Saruman bred the Uruk-hai by crossing Orcs with goblin-men in the pits beneath Isengard's ring wall.
The palantír of Orthanc corrupted Saruman when Sauron used it to gradually dominate his will and counsel.
Saruman's voice possesses an enchanting power of persuasion that can sway even the wisest of listeners.
The Uruk-hai are a superior breed of Orc that can march in daylight without the weakness suffered by other Orcs.
Isengard is a great fortress complex with a ring wall of stone enclosing gardens, forges, and underground caverns.
Saruman ripped up the ancient trees around Isengard to fuel his furnaces and build war machines for his army.
The White Hand of Saruman is the emblem painted on the shields and helmets of his Uruk-hai soldiers.
Saruman studied ring-lore extensively in the archives of Gondor, hoping to discover the secret of making Rings of Power.
The Ents destroyed Isengard by breaking the dam on the River Isen and flooding the entire ring of the fortress.
Saruman imprisoned Gandalf atop the pinnacle of Orthanc until the eagle Gwaihir rescued him.
Saruman's original color was white, signifying his status as the highest-ranking wizard among the Istari.
After his defeat, Saruman adopted the title 'Saruman of Many Colours' to show he had surpassed the White.
Saruman's army of ten thousand Uruk-hai marched to Helm's Deep to destroy the people of Rohan.
Wormtongue served as Saruman's spy in Edoras, poisoning King Théoden's mind to weaken Rohan from within.
Saruman sought the One Ring for himself, hoping to use it to overthrow Sauron and rule Middle-earth.
The pits of Isengard are underground caverns where Saruman's Orcs forge weapons and breed new soldiers.
Saruman sent his Uruk-hai scouts to capture the hobbits, believing one of them carried the One Ring.
The fires of Isengard burned day and night as Saruman transformed the fortress into an industrial war machine.
Saruman's staff was broken by Gandalf the White at the gates of Isengard, stripping him of his wizardly power.
Treebeard recognized Saruman as a threat after discovering that Orcs from Isengard were cutting down Fangorn's trees.
Saruman eventually took over the Shire under the alias Sharkey, industrializing it and oppressing the hobbits.
The Uruk-hai could run for three days without rest, carrying captured hobbits from Amon Hen toward Isengard.
Saruman's knowledge of mechanical devices and explosive fire exceeded that of any other being in Middle-earth.
The Isen river flows past Isengard and was diverted by the Ents to flood Saruman's underground works.
Saruman was originally a Maia spirit named Curumo, sent by the Valar to aid the free peoples against Sauron.
The seeing-stone of Orthanc allowed Saruman to communicate directly with Sauron in the Dark Tower of Barad-dûr.
Saruman created an explosive blasting fire that breached the walls of Helm's Deep during the siege.
Gríma Wormtongue killed Saruman at Bag End by slitting his throat, ending the wizard's diminished existence.
Saruman's army included wild men from Dunland who bore ancient grudges against the Rohirrim horse-lords.
The Uruk-hai leader Uglúk commanded the troop that captured Merry and Pippin at the breaking of the Fellowship.
Saruman's orcs bore the mark of the White Hand while Mordor's orcs bore the Red Eye of Sauron.
Orthanc contains a secret chamber at its pinnacle where Saruman kept the palantír and conducted his sorcery.
Saruman studied pipe-weed cultivation and secretly traded with the Shire, stockpiling supplies for his conquest.
The Ent-draught that Merry and Pippin drank was water drawn from the living springs tended by Treebeard in Fangorn.
Saruman convinced himself that alliance with Sauron was pragmatic wisdom rather than submission to evil.
The Ring of Orthanc was the circular stone wall surrounding Isengard, once beautiful gardens now turned to slag.
Lurtz was the first Uruk-hai to emerge from the pits of Isengard and led the attack at Amon Hen.
Saruman's mechanical knowledge allowed him to create war engines, siege ladders, and battering rams for his army.
The floodwaters released by the Ents drowned Saruman's underground forges and rendered his war industry useless.
Saruman's defeat at Isengard was accomplished entirely by the Ents, without any human army attacking.
Saruman locked himself in Orthanc after the flooding, as the Ents could not break the ancient stone tower.
Gandalf offered Saruman a chance to repent and be free after the destruction of Isengard, but Saruman refused.
Wormtongue threw the palantír from the top of Orthanc, inadvertently giving it to Gandalf and the Company.
Saruman's Uruk-hai carried the White Hand banner and iron shields bearing his device into battle.
The caverns beneath Isengard extended deep underground, housing armories, breeding pits, and storerooms.
Saruman's spies included crows called crebain from Dunland that scouted the movements of the Fellowship.
The old Ent Quickbeam was the first to grow angry when he learned Saruman's orcs had destroyed rowan trees.
Saruman's duplicity was first suspected when Gandalf visited Isengard and was offered a share of power.
Saruman attempted to delay and divide the White Council to prevent them from driving Sauron out of Dol Guldur.
The Ents marched on Isengard after the Entmoot, the first time they had gone to war in many ages of the world.
Saruman's deep knowledge of crafting and metallurgy made his war machines more devastating than simple Orc weapons.
The berserker Uruk-hai carried explosive charges into the culvert at Helm's Deep to breach the Deeping Wall.
Saruman's fall from grace was the greatest betrayal among the Istari, as he was entrusted with the highest mission.
Isengard's ring wall had only one gate, a great arch of iron through which Saruman's armies marched to war.
The Huorn trees that Treebeard sent destroyed the remnants of Saruman's army fleeing from Helm's Deep.
Saruman could forge crude imitations of Elven crafts using his deep understanding of ring-lore and metallurgy.
Pippin looked into the palantír of Orthanc and was briefly contacted by Sauron who mistook him for the Ring-bearer.
Saruman's industrialization of Isengard involved strip-mining the earth, polluting the river, and felling ancient forests.
The Wizard's Vale around Isengard was once a green and pleasant place before Saruman turned it into wasteland.
Saruman kept wolves as war beasts in Isengard, bred larger and more fierce than ordinary Warg mounts.
The Entmoot debated for three days before Treebeard led the Ents in their march against Isengard.
Saruman's eyes were described as having a strange gleam, deep and penetrating, that reflected his cunning mind.
Saruman's orcs used iron wheels and pulleys to raise supplies from the deep pits to the surface of Isengard.
Wormtongue brought Saruman intelligence from Rohan about troop movements, defenses, and Théoden's declining health.
Saruman believed he could find the One Ring in the Gladden Fields where Isildur lost it centuries ago.
The crossing of Orcs and Men in Isengard's pits was considered one of Saruman's most abominable acts.
Saruman's Uruk-hai carried specially prepared provisions of man-flesh and a burning liquor for long marches.
The tower of Orthanc has four pillars that meet at a peak, with a flat space atop used as an observation platform.
Saruman's cloak appeared white from a distance but up close shimmered with all colors woven into its fabric.
The River Isen's fords were the main entry point from Isengard into Rohan, and Saruman's forces fought to control them.
Théodred, son of Théoden, was killed at the Fords of Isen by Saruman's forces before the siege of Helm's Deep.
Saruman used his knowledge of herbalism and alchemy to create potent poisons and medicinal compounds.
Gandalf the White was sent back specifically to replace Saruman's role after the fallen wizard betrayed his order.
The Ents' assault on Isengard was so thorough that they tore apart the stone ring wall with their bare hands.
Saruman's secret dealings with the Shire involved buying pipe-weed through Lotho Sackville-Baggins as intermediary.
Under Saruman's rule as Sharkey, the Shire's mill was expanded into a polluting factory that fouled the water.
Saruman had studied in Valinor under the Vala Aulë, gaining deep knowledge of crafting and the physical world.
Treebeard described Saruman as having a mind of metal and wheels, no longer caring for growing things.
Saruman's army included half-orcs who could pass as ugly Men and served as spies among human settlements.
The destruction of Isengard left only Orthanc standing, surrounded by steaming pools and rubble from the Ents' wrath.
Saruman's death at Bag End was witnessed by the returning hobbits, after which a grey mist rose from his body.
When Saruman died, a thin grey spirit rose from his body and drifted westward but was blown away by a cold wind.
Saruman's Uruk-hai wore thick iron armor and carried broad-bladed swords designed for hacking through shield walls.
Lotho Sackville-Baggins was Saruman's puppet ruler of the Shire before Saruman arrived and took direct control.
Saruman's control over Théoden was maintained through Wormtongue's constant whispering and herbal poisons.
The caverns of Isengard contained underground streams that powered water-wheels driving Saruman's machinery.
Saruman ordered the systematic deforestation of Fangorn's borders, which finally provoked the Ents into action.
The Uruk-hai scouts Grishnákh and Uglúk quarreled over whether to take the captured hobbits to Isengard or Mordor.
Saruman's banner bore a white hand on a black field, a deliberate inversion of Gondor's white tree on black.
The palantír revealed to Saruman the military strength of Mordor, making resistance seem futile compared to alliance.
Saruman's rule of the Shire was called the Scouring, and it ended when the four hobbits led a rebellion against him.
Isengard's underground tunnels connected to natural cave systems that extended deep beneath the Misty Mountains.
Saruman tried to waylay Frodo and the Ring-bearers on the road home but was already a diminished, powerless figure.
The Dunlendings who served Saruman were promised the lands of Rohan in exchange for fighting alongside the Uruk-hai.
Saruman's treachery was revealed to the full Council only when Gandalf escaped Orthanc and reported to Elrond.
The keys of Orthanc were surrendered to Treebeard after the Ents' victory, and the tower became the Ents' prisoner.
Saruman used his deep voice and persuasive speech to nearly convince Théoden and Gandalf to release him from Orthanc.
The stone of Orthanc was so hard that even the Ents' great strength could not crack or damage the tower.
Saruman engineered explosive mines filled with a fire of his own devising, a technology unknown to other peoples.
After the War of the Ring, Saruman walked from Isengard to the Shire on foot, having lost all his former power.
The crossing at the Fords of Isen was strategically essential for Saruman's invasion of Rohan's heartland.
Saruman's once-white robes became stained and filthy during his diminished exile, reflecting his fallen state.
The Ents kept watch over the locked tower of Orthanc for years after the War, creating a new tree-garden around it.
Saruman's research into the Rings of Power was the most extensive of any scholar in the Third Age of Middle-earth.
The battering ram Grond was named after Morgoth's great hammer and was used by Sauron's forces at Minas Tirith.
Saruman's army crossed the Gap of Rohan to reach Helm's Deep, marching through the night to arrive at dawn.
The wild men of Dunland painted their faces and bore crude weapons alongside Saruman's better-equipped Uruk-hai.
Saruman kept detailed records and maps of Middle-earth's geography, resources, and strategic positions in Orthanc.
The destruction of the One Ring ultimately led to the fading of all Rings of Power, including Saruman's lesser works.
Saruman's greatest asset was his vast scholarly knowledge, which he corrupted to serve his desire for domination.
Wormtongue was promised Éowyn as a reward for his service to Saruman, feeding his obsessive desire for her.
The Huorn-wood that trapped the Orcs after Helm's Deep was directed by Treebeard under the Ents' command.
Saruman's spirit was denied return to Valinor after death, condemned to wander as a formless shade forever.
The pits of Isengard produced hundreds of crude crossbows and heavy shields for the Dunlending infantry.
Saruman's Uruk-hai could track prey for leagues through wilderness using a combination of scent and discipline.
The iron gates of Isengard were wrought by Gondorian craftsmen long before Saruman claimed the fortress.
Saruman maintained a personal guard of half-orc soldiers who attended him even in his exile to the Shire.
The old road through the Gap of Rohan passes directly beneath the shadow of Orthanc at Isengard's gate.
